jokes.text: What are these sad print-outs?
jokes.setup: Moira sees Johnny's research materials
jokes.punchline: Dismisses them as 'sad' with typical dramatic flair
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moira voice - instantly dismissive and theatrical. 'Sad print-outs' is economical character work.

jokes.text: Are the police auctioning off crime scene vehicles?
jokes.setup: Moira sees car listings and jumps to worst-case scenario
jokes.punchline: Assumes the cars must be from crime scenes
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific Moira logic - why would cars be cheap unless they're tainted? Perfect character integration.

jokes.text: I really don't see myself driving any of these. / I would rather drive a scooter. And we all know how I feel about scooters, those... stupid helmets.
jokes.setup: David's initial rejection of the car options
jokes.punchline: Escalates to rejecting scooters purely based on helmet aesthetics
jokes.explanation: Classic David - rejecting practical transportation for purely aesthetic reasons. The 'stupid helmets' is perfect character voice.

jokes.text: Does this site have boats?
jokes.setup: Family discussing buying a car from online listings
jokes.punchline: Moira completely ignores the car discussion and asks about boats
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy - one line that shows Moira's complete disconnect from practical reality. Maximum comedy per word.

jokes.text: Is that a spreadsheet? / Yes, yes, it is.
jokes.setup: Johnny presents his organized proposal
jokes.punchline: David's horror at the spreadsheet, Johnny's proud confirmation
jokes.explanation: Great character contrast - Johnny's business-like approach vs David's aesthetic sensibilities.

jokes.text: We don't even have the car yet! / Okay, we're just trying to work within your schedule.
jokes.setup: Family arguing over car scheduling before they own a car
jokes.punchline: David treats Johnny's reasonable point as a scheduling issue
jokes.explanation: Classic family dysfunction - fighting over hypothetical car usage with mock-consideration.

jokes.text: But, sometimes your choices are less suited to a family's needs, and more suited to a pony-tailed Lothario, cruising down the Monte Carlo coast. Or like um, Vin Diesel.
jokes.setup: Moira commenting on Johnny's car-buying history
jokes.punchline: Hyper-specific imagery followed by the mundane Vin Diesel reference
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira - florid language ('pony-tailed Lothario') crashes into pop culture reference. The contrast is brilliantly executed.

jokes.text: Not that we can afford a car that says a lot about us
jokes.setup: Moira talking about cars reflecting personality
jokes.punchline: Self-aware admission of their financial limitations
jokes.explanation: Nice self-awareness from Moira about their situation, delivered with characteristic eloquence.

jokes.text: You bought me this dress, David. / I know! And it cost twice as much as the car you wanna buy.
jokes.setup: David criticizing Moira's expensive outfit for car shopping
jokes.punchline: David realizes the irony of his own criticism
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic self-own by David. The math of dress vs. car cost highlights their financial disconnect.

jokes.text: Yeah, says the moving target in the Bespoke suit.
jokes.setup: Johnny claiming he won't just wear a suit
jokes.punchline: David calls him a 'moving target' for his expensive clothing
jokes.explanation: 'Moving target' is a clever way to describe someone who stands out for being overdressed. Sharp David wit.

jokes.text: to mimic the trappings of a workaday woman. / And if you'll notice, David, no cufflinks. / Wow! I am blinded by the disenfranchisement.
jokes.setup: Moira describing her costume change, pointing out no cufflinks
jokes.punchline: David's sarcastic response using 'disenfranchisement' ironically
jokes.explanation: Peak Schitt's Creek wordplay. 'Blinded by the disenfranchisement' is brilliantly over-the-top sarcasm that perfectly captures David's voice.

jokes.text: Hey, sorry I'm late, there was very heavy foot traffic on the way over.
jokes.setup: Alexis arriving late to work
jokes.punchline: Blames 'heavy foot traffic' in small town Schitt's Creek
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alexis - applying big city problems to tiny town reality. Shows her disconnect from current circumstances.

jokes.text: Who is this cool cat, and what did he do with Ted?
jokes.setup: Ted appears with mysterious injuries
jokes.punchline: Alexis's playful reaction treating him like a tough guy
jokes.explanation: 'Cool cat' is charmingly dated slang that fits Alexis's affected speech patterns.

jokes.text: Did you get in a bar fight?
jokes.setup: Ted's mysterious injuries
jokes.punchline: Alexis assumes the most dramatic explanation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alexis's tendency toward dramatic assumptions, but fairly predictable.

jokes.text: You owe someone money. / Alexis, I pay off my credit card bills two weeks in advance, what do you think?
jokes.setup: Alexis guessing why Ted is being secretive
jokes.punchline: Ted's incredibly responsible financial habits as character reveal
jokes.explanation: Great character contrast - Alexis assumes drama, Ted is boringly responsible. The specificity of 'two weeks in advance' sells it.

jokes.text: I know that they are for the animals, but I find that when I get tired, just one or two kind of help me focus.
jokes.setup: Ted finally explaining his secretive behavior
jokes.punchline: Admits to taking animal medication for focus
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly uncomfortable admission that explains Ted's secrecy. The casual 'one or two' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: did someone die, or are we going through like, a mid-life Goth phase?
jokes.setup: Stevie asking about the dark lighting
jokes.punchline: Offers two explanations: death or mid-life Goth phase
jokes.explanation: Classic Stevie deadpan. 'Mid-life Goth phase' is a funny specific alternative to death.

jokes.text: Someone died. / Okay, I'm just finding the bulbs to make my skin look very jaundice-y.
jokes.setup: David confirms someone died
jokes.punchline: Stevie continues complaining about the lighting despite the death
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie - even death doesn't stop her from complaining about unflattering lighting. Great character priority reveal.

jokes.text: Okay, and now I can't tell whether you're playing into the joke, or whether um... you're telling the truth. / She was 78.
jokes.setup: Stevie unsure if David is joking about death
jokes.punchline: David provides specific age detail, confirming it's real
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy as Stevie realizes she's been insensitive. The age detail makes it uncomfortably specific.

jokes.text: Why, you didn't kill her.
jokes.setup: Stevie apologizing for her insensitive comments
jokes.punchline: David's matter-of-fact response dismissing her guilt
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery. The economy is excellent - maximum impact with minimum words.

jokes.text: I mean, she was a mess, but I liked her. That's probably why I liked her.
jokes.setup: David describing his relationship with his deceased aunt
jokes.punchline: Admits he liked her because she was a mess
jokes.explanation: Great self-awareness from David about his attraction to chaotic people. Shows character depth through humor.

jokes.text: I'd like you to finish that sentence.
jokes.setup: Stevie starts to offer help reluctantly
jokes.punchline: David calls out her obvious reluctance
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment as David forces Stevie to confront her insincerity. Typical David directness.

jokes.text: I don't think you could handle it. This whole thing is super depressing.
jokes.setup: David accepting Stevie's help offer
jokes.punchline: Immediately tells her she can't handle it because it's depressing
jokes.explanation: Perfect David contradiction - asks for help then immediately dismisses the helper. Classic character consistency.

jokes.text: Oh, sorry, we don't want any. / I'm just joking. Come on in! / No, I'm not!
jokes.setup: Roland answering door, appearing to turn Johnny away
jokes.punchline: Says he's joking, then immediately says he's not joking
jokes.explanation: Classic Roland move - the double reversal captures his obnoxious but ultimately harmless nature.

jokes.text: and Fridays, it's strip crib. / Well, thank God it's not Friday. / Well, we can pretend.
jokes.setup: Roland mentions strip cribbage, Johnny relieved it's not Friday
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn suggests they pretend it's Friday
jokes.explanation: Good escalation - Johnny thinks he's safe, then Jocelyn makes it worse. Classic sitcom structure.

jokes.text: But you don't have any money. / Oh, I know I don't have any money, but I need to look like I don't have money.
jokes.setup: Johnny explaining he needs to look poor for car shopping
jokes.punchline: Roland points out the obvious paradox
jokes.explanation: Great paradox joke - Johnny's convoluted logic exposed by Roland's simple observation. The repetition emphasizes the absurdity.

jokes.text: Here's what I'm hearing you say, what you're saying is you wanna dress like me, because I look like I don't have any money!
jokes.setup: Roland interpreting Johnny's request for casual clothes
jokes.punchline: Roland proudly realizes Johnny wants to look poor like him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Roland - completely missing the insult and turning it into a compliment. Shows his obliviousness.

jokes.text: What do you want, uh PVC, or a Hawaiian shirt?
jokes.setup: Roland offering 'hip' clothing options
jokes.punchline: Suggests PVC or Hawaiian shirt as hip alternatives
jokes.explanation: Funny contrast between two equally unhip options. Shows Roland's complete misunderstanding of fashion.

jokes.text: Now unfortunately, we haven't quite found your Aunt Maureen's ashes, but, we know they're here somewhere.
jokes.setup: Professional funeral director explaining the situation
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions they've lost the ashes
jokes.explanation: Great dark comedy - the casual delivery of devastating news. The 'somewhere' makes it worse.

jokes.text: The problem is they all come in from the crematorium on the same day, and sometimes, they're not labeled.
jokes.setup: Explaining the lost ashes situation
jokes.punchline: Reveals systematic labeling problems with cremated remains
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly horrifying detail about funeral home operations. The casual bureaucratic tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: That side of my family has a bit of a reputation for conflict. / And philandering. / And fraud. / And gun-play.
jokes.setup: David explaining his family background
jokes.punchline: Escalating list of criminal activities ending with 'gun-play'
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation structure building to 'gun-play.' Each item gets more serious, delivered with characteristic David matter-of-factness.

jokes.text: Found them. They were on top of the microwave.
jokes.setup: The missing ashes situation
jokes.punchline: Ashes found in mundane kitchen location
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy payoff. The mundane location (microwave) makes the situation absurdly casual.

jokes.text: Can you get us some whitener for the coffees? / Oh... what have I been using?
jokes.setup: Funeral director asking for coffee whitener
jokes.punchline: David realizes what he might have been using instead
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark implication that David may have been using cremated remains as coffee whitener. The horror of realization is perfect.

jokes.text: Well, if I squint, I feel like I'm seeing your rough and rugged country cousin.
jokes.setup: Moira evaluating Johnny's outfit change
jokes.punchline: Describes him as a 'rough and rugged country cousin'
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira - backhanded compliment delivered with theatrical flair. 'If I squint' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: I apprenticed costume design under Stan LaCoulier.
jokes.setup: Johnny questioning Moira's outfit choice
jokes.punchline: Moira name-dropping a likely made-up costume designer
jokes.explanation: Classic Moira fabrication to justify her decisions

jokes.text: Well, I don't remember the 'Everything Be Irie' bumper sticker.
jokes.setup: Johnny responding to Moira's nostalgic car memory
jokes.punchline: Points out the obvious difference - a Jamaican bumper sticker
jokes.explanation: Good practical Johnny response to Moira's romanticism. The specific bumper sticker text is a nice detail.

jokes.text: Yes, especially with this being our first ever purchase of an automobile. / Yes, it's our first car, let's hope we can afford it.
jokes.setup: Johnny and Moira acting poor for the salesman
jokes.punchline: Moira's overly formal language betrays their act
jokes.explanation: Good cringe as Moira can't help but speak formally even while pretending to be poor. Classic character consistency.

jokes.text: Yes, my poor 'usband, lost his job recently as a tennis pro, yes, at a public resort, that is, yeah.
jokes.setup: Moira continuing the poor act with fake accent
jokes.punchline: Drops her H's while describing Johnny as a tennis pro at a 'public' resort
jokes.explanation: Peak Moira cringe - the fake accent combined with still describing upper-class jobs. The stammering 'yeah' at the end is perfect.

jokes.text: It'll be nice to get off the streets, and be indoors for a change, yeah?
jokes.setup: Moira continuing her poor person act
jokes.punchline: Implies they're homeless while still using formal speech patterns
jokes.explanation: Escalation of Moira's act to claiming homelessness, but her posh cadence makes it obviously fake.

jokes.text: We've-we've struggled with penury for quite some time now, well, just two years ago, we were practically 'omeless.
jokes.setup: Moira trying to sound poor while using big words
jokes.punchline: Uses 'penury' while dropping H's, claims to be 'practically homeless'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Moira moment - 'penury' is an extremely formal word for poverty, delivered with dropped H's. The contradiction is perfect.

jokes.text: I was one of two identical twins. Tragically, I was snatched from my crib at birth by Russian mobsters.
jokes.setup: Salesman asks where they're from, Moira launches into backstory
jokes.punchline: Creates elaborate fiction about being kidnapped by Russian mobsters as a baby
jokes.explanation: Peak absurd Moira storytelling. The specificity of 'Russian mobsters' and 'identical twins' setup creates elaborate fiction instantly.

jokes.text: Well, if you're identical, I thought you'd both be valuable. / Yes, wouldn't you? She wasn't born yet. / 'Til three minutes later, and the Bratva work very quickly.
jokes.setup: Salesman points out logical flaw in twin story
jokes.punchline: Moira doubles down with ridiculous timing details and 'Bratva' reference
jokes.explanation: Masterful improvisation - when caught in plot hole, Moira creates even more absurd details. 'Bratva work very quickly' is brilliant.

jokes.text: 'Til three minutes later, and the Bratva work very quickly.
jokes.setup: Explaining how identical twin wasn't born during kidnapping
jokes.punchline: Moira using real Russian mob terminology while making up story
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - Moira's knowledge of Russian mob terms makes absurd story more ridiculous

jokes.text: No one's gonna know that you're having an adventurous little rendezvous with an apple-cheeked farm-woman!
jokes.setup: Alexis thinks she understands Ted's secret
jokes.punchline: Creates romantic fiction about Ted and the farmer
jokes.explanation: 'Apple-cheeked farm-woman' is perfectly specific Alexis language. Shows her tendency to romanticize everything.

jokes.text: I was making a house call to Lisa's farm, and got head-butted by her goat.
jokes.setup: Ted finally reveals what really happened
jokes.punchline: Mundane reality - he was attacked by a goat
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the mystery setup. The mundane reality is funnier than Alexis's romantic fantasy.

jokes.text: Is that when one of you is on the floor, and then the other one kind of just like hops...
jokes.setup: Ted explains he was head-butted by a goat
jokes.punchline: Alexis still thinks it's a sexual position
jokes.explanation: Good cringe as Alexis doubles down on her misunderstanding. The trailing off mid-description is perfect.

jokes.text: Um, so what are... where-how... Where should we do... Well, I don't want people parking on her!
jokes.setup: Deciding where to scatter ashes in parking lot
jokes.punchline: David's concern about people parking on the ashes
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about the practical concerns of ash scattering

jokes.text: So I just need to take up palm reading, get a few cats, move to Saskatchewan. Well, a girl could dream.
jokes.setup: Stevie describing her aunt's life trajectory
jokes.punchline: Presents this depressing path as aspirational with 'a girl could dream'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie deadpan - presenting a nightmare future as a dream. The specific details make it funnier.

jokes.text: And if they're not, I'll be sure to scatter your ashes in a much nicer parking lot.
jokes.setup: David encouraging Stevie about her future choices
jokes.punchline: Promises better ashes disposal location as gesture of friendship
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy friendship moment - David's version of comfort is promising better ash disposal. Perfect character voice.

jokes.text: My wife was addicted to your show! / Well, I find it's wonderful, but we don't have a television, so.
jokes.setup: Salesman's wife recognizes Moira from her soap opera
jokes.punchline: Moira maintains poor act by claiming no television
jokes.explanation: Good cringe as Moira tries to maintain character while being recognized. The television lie is perfectly Moira.

jokes.text: Or perhaps we might have to take our business to a dealership that sells brand new cars. / As you can see, we can't even afford her meds right now.
jokes.setup: Moira threatens to go to expensive dealership
jokes.punchline: Johnny immediately undercuts her by referencing her 'meds'
jokes.explanation: Great marital comedy - Johnny completely undermines Moira's negotiation stance. Good escalation structure.

jokes.text: Heather Warner doesn't actually have a cat.
jokes.setup: Alexis offering to deliver cat flea shampoo
jokes.punchline: Ted reveals there is no cat
jokes.explanation: Good setup payoff structure. The economy is excellent - maximum revelation with minimum words.

jokes.text: She like, thinks she has a cat? That's so sad. But I guess she is like 50, so that makes sense. / Yep, she's 42. And she's not crazy. It's... code.
jokes.setup: Alexis assumes mental illness, gets age wrong
jokes.punchline: Ted corrects age and reveals it's code for something else
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - Alexis's assumptions (age, mental state) are all wrong. Ted's pause before 'code' builds suspense.

jokes.text: A booty call?! / Well, I'm not crazy about the term, but...
jokes.setup: Alexis realizes the 'cat' is code for hookup
jokes.punchline: Ted's embarrassed confirmation while objecting to crude terminology
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Ted's prudishness. The reluctant confirmation is perfectly Ted.

jokes.text: Ted, look at you, Little Sexy Susan!
jokes.setup: Alexis praising Ted's romantic life
jokes.punchline: Calls him 'Little Sexy Susan' with enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: 'Little Sexy Susan' is brilliantly absurd - perfectly Alexis mix of encouragement and bizarre nickname choice.

jokes.text: So we're drinking to me not becoming an alcoholic? / Mhmm. / Off to a good start.
jokes.setup: David toasts to Stevie not following her aunt's path
jokes.punchline: Stevie notes the irony of drinking to not becoming an alcoholic
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy irony - drinking to sobriety. Stevie's deadpan delivery of the obvious contradiction is excellent.

jokes.text: That is the deed to the motel. / Sorry? / Maureen Budd owned the motel.
jokes.setup: Lawyer presenting inheritance documents
jokes.punchline: Reveals Stevie has inherited the entire motel
jokes.explanation: Good plot twist moment. The casual delivery of life-changing news adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: Was she of sound mind?!
jokes.setup: Stevie processing that she's inherited a motel
jokes.punchline: Desperately questions aunt's mental state twice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Stevie reaction - the exclamation point delivery shows her panic at becoming tied to the motel.

jokes.text: You look like a contestant on 'The Price Is Right.'
jokes.setup: David sees his parents in their car-buying outfits
jokes.punchline: Compares them to game show contestants
jokes.explanation: Good specific reference that captures their overly enthusiastic, slightly desperate appearance.

jokes.text: $6,000 quid, love.
jokes.setup: Moira revealing how much they paid for the car
jokes.punchline: Uses 'quid' while still in fake accent character
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the fake accent. Shows Moira still partially in character even back home.

jokes.text: You can't put a price on dignity. / Uh, tell that to your outfit.
jokes.setup: Moira defending their overpayment for dignity
jokes.punchline: David points out she compromised dignity with her outfit
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline structure. David's comeback completely undermines Moira's dignity argument.

jokes.text: This is your outfit. / What?!
jokes.setup: David criticizing Moira's outfit
jokes.punchline: Moira realizes David picked the outfit he's now criticizing
jokes.explanation: Great escalation as the layers of irony unfold. Moira's horrified 'What?!' is perfect as she realizes David's hypocrisy.

jokes.text: Were all the other cars on fire, then?
jokes.setup: David seeing the terrible car his parents bought
jokes.punchline: Suggests fire would be the only excuse for choosing this car
jokes.explanation: Perfect David hyperbole. The specificity of 'on fire' as the only acceptable excuse is brilliantly excessive.
